answer:
**The corpus contains one work of Old English elegies: *Old English Elegies*, an anthology of 17 short poems.**[^1]

Several of these elegies evoke the Anglo-Saxon *comitatus* ideal of loyalty between a man (often a thane or retainer) and his lord (*dryhten*, gold-friend, ring-giver, or treasure-giver), typically through themes of loss, exile (*wræcsith*), and lament after the lord's death or separation. The most explicit examples are:

- **The Wanderer** (ch. 11): The lone wanderer (*anhaga*) mourns his dead "gold-friend" (lord), from whom he is exiled after the hall and retainers are destroyed. He recalls embracing his "liege-lord" (*hlaford*), longing for past counsel and gifts at the "gift-throne" (*giefstol*), and notes how "hall-warriors" and "proud young thanes" served such lords before *wyrd* (fate) scattered them.[^2][^3]
  
- **Deor** (ch. 13): The poet (*scop*) Deor laments his displacement from the "Heodenings'" court, where he had been "dear to my lord" (*dryhten leof*) with a good following, until a rival took his place and *land-riht* (land-right). The refrain "That passed over; so may this" (*Þæs ofereode, þisses swa mæg*) frames sorrows including lost lordship.[^4]

Other elegies allude more obliquely:
- **The Seafarer** (ch. 5) notes no man has "a lord so dear" (*dryhten swa deore*) without anxiety over fate.[^5]
- **Maxims I** (ch. 6) proverbially describes a queen passing the cup first to "her lord's hand" (*hlafordes hand*) amid retainers.[^6]
- **The Wife's Lament** (ch. 3) depicts a woman's separation from *her* lord due to kinsmen's feuding, but the focus is female.[^7]

No other Old English works in the corpus (e.g., Cynewulf's *Andreas*, *Elene*, *Juliana*) are elegies or center this theme.[^8]

[^1]: *Old English Elegies*, [work](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/).
[^2]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 11 (*The Wanderer*), [p-9462cc](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/11-the-wanderer/translation/#p-9462cc).
[^3]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 11 (*The Wanderer*), [full chapter](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/11-the-wanderer/translation/).
[^4]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 13 (*Deor*), [p-6b7e51](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/13-welund-him-be-wurman-wraeces-cunnade/translation/#p-6b7e51).
[^5]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 5 (*The Seafarer*), [p-549618](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/05-maey-ich-be-me-sylfum-soth-yied-wrekan-sithas-seya/translation/#p-549618).
[^6]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 6 (*Maxims I*), [p-84cf09](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/06-maxims-i/translation/#p-84cf09).
[^7]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 3 (*The Wife's Lament*), [full chapter](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/03-ik-this-giedd-wreche-bi-me-ful-geomorre/translation/).
[^8]: Search results across *Andreas*, *Elene*, *Juliana* yielded only incidental "thane" mentions, not elegiac man-lord relations.
